coredata.dc:title: Smooth-surface octahedral Zn2SnO4 sensitized by carbon quantum dot for enhanced triethylamine detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Zhenyu
Sui, Yongchen
Guo, Fengzhu
Liu, Panpan
Meng, Fanli
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the combination of octahedral Zn2SnO4 with the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is pioneered for the efficient detection of triethylamine. CQDs-modified Zn2SnO4 materials were synthesized to perform rapid detection of trace triethylamine present in air. In the gas test, the optimal composite's response value to triethylamine is 600% higher than the value of the original Zn2SnO4 to triethylamine, and the tendency of selective to triethylamine is more than ten times than other kinds of gases and possesses a short response/recovery times of 18 s/29 s. The exceptional properties of the composite are attributed to the superb electron transport capacity brought by the modification of 5 nm carbon quantum dots. The novel material will solve the issues of slow reaction time and low response of previous materials in detecting triethylamine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Zn2SnO4
Octahedral
Π-states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD diffractograms of Zn2SnO4 and different composites.

(a) XPS spectrum of 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composite, (b) Sn 3d spectrum, (c) Zn 2p spectrum, (d) O1s spectrum of Zn2SnO4, (e) O1s spectrum of 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4, (f) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4.
(a), (b) UV–Vis energy band analysis plots of Zn2SnO4 and 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4, (c) FTIR analysis plots of 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composites and pristine Zn2SnO4.
SEM of Zn2SnO4, (b), (c), (d) TEM of CQDs, (e), (f) are the mapping of all elements, (g), (h), (i), (j) is the mapping localization of the Zn, Sn, O, C.

(a) Resistance curves of pure Zn2SnO4 and different ratios of composites with temperature changing, (b) resistance curves of the best composite and original Zn2SnO4 at 270 °C, (c) Electrical characterization (I–V characteristics) of the best composite under different conditions.
(a) Response curve and fitting curve of 1 wt% CQDs@Zn2SnO4 at 270 °C, (b) concentration gradient response curves at 270 °C.
(a) Selectivity curves of sensors for different types of gases, (b) selectivity histogram comparison at 270 °C.
(a) Long-term stability of the sensor over 30 days, (b) four repetitions of sensor response performance to 100 ppm TEA, the sensor operating temperature is 270 °C.
Procedure for the reaction of CQDs@Zn2SnO4 material with oxygen and triethylamine.
Electron leap in the energy band.
Performance of the 100 ppm TEA sensors based on different sensing materials.

head.ce:title: Smooth-surface octahedral Zn2SnO4 sensitized by carbon quantum dot for enhanced triethylamine detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the combination of octahedral Zn2SnO4 with the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is pioneered for the efficient detection of triethylamine. CQDs-modified Zn2SnO4 materials were synthesized to perform rapid detection of trace triethylamine present in air. In the gas test, the optimal composite's response value to triethylamine is 600% higher than the value of the original Zn2SnO4 to triethylamine, and the tendency of selective to triethylamine is more than ten times than other kinds of gases and possesses a short response/recovery times of 18 s/29 s. The exceptional properties of the composite are attributed to the superb electron transport capacity brought by the modification of 5 nm carbon quantum dots. The novel material will solve the issues of slow reaction time and low response of previous materials in detecting triethylamine.

ce:section.ce:para: Triethylamine, also known as TEA, usually used as a solvent and curing agent, and preservative in industrial production due to its special chemical properties [1,2]. TEA has a pungent odor and is volatile in nature. Triethylamine in air at a concentration of more than 10 ppm is dangerous [3]. If it evaporates in a limited space, it can enter the human body through breathing and skin contact, which may cause damage to the respiratory tract and central system, and More seriously, it may cause mucosal corrosion and chemical burns to organs [4–6]. Therefore, how to detect the volatile triethylamine is a work of great significance. In previous research, gas chromatography and other methods are often used for quantitatively detecting triethylamine [7]. However, due to its high cost and high detection limit in performance, it is more suitable for the detection of triethylamine in high concentrations. Therefore, researching the low-cost and high-efficiency trace triethylamine gas sensors is important [8–10].

The mainstream gas sensors in the market today are mainly metal oxide (MO) semiconductors, electrochemical materials, infrared gas sensors, and so on. Among them, metal oxide semiconductor materials have more than half of the market share because of their better stability, low detection limit and small size. However, original metal oxide semiconductor materials have poor selectivity, undesirable response value, and long response/recovery times in gas detection. Many researchers tried various methods to modify original metal oxide semiconductor materials, such as combining two or more types of materials to form heterojunctions [11–13], precious metal doping [14–16], synthetic porous structures [17,18].

Quantum dots have dimensions of 10 nm or less. They have gradually become the favorite for materials science research with their excellent physical and chemical properties. In the energy area, there are numerous studies on solar cells with calcium titanium ore quantum dots [19]; In photocatalysis, the non-polluting nature and excellent catalytic properties of CQDs are driving the research progress in this area [20–23]; Nevertheless, the research of CQDs for the modification to gas sensors is relatively rare [24]. The above work shows that combining carbon quantum dots' excellent electron transfer ability and quantum size effect with sensitive materials for gas sensors would be a direction with research value.

The innovation of this work is that the first to use a composite of carbon quantum dots and binary metal oxides to detect triethylamine. Zn2SnO4 with an octahedral shape was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method [25]. CQDs@Zn2SnO4 composites were synthesized by stirring and ultrasonication. Compared with the original Zn2SnO4 material, fewer response times can be achieved because of the special electron transfer in composite materials. The improved response value is due to the modification of quantum dots to enlarge the material's surface area. The new composite material is capable of accomplishing rapid and accurate sensor indicators for monitoring trace amounts of triethylamine. The raw materials of CQDs prepared in the study are widely available, which also greatly reduces the cost of the sensors. It provides a feasible idea for future research on CQDs' type sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Zn2SnO4, CQDs, CQDs@Zn2SnO4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mmol ZnSO4–7H2O and 0.5 mmol SnCl4–5H2O were dissolved in 40 ml DI water and stirred for 20 min, and 0.08 g CTAB and 6 mmol NaOH were added in the solution, stirring for 10 min. Then, the homogeneous white turbid solution into the reaction kettle and heated at 200 °C for 24 h, and after washing by centrifugation, dried the reaction product at 60 °C for 24 h, after that, calcined the product in air at 500 °C for 2 h.

0.4 g l-ascorbic acid was added into solution of 6 ml DI water and 4 ml ethylene glycol with stirring vigorously for 30 min. Heated the solution at 160 °C for 70 min to obtain yellow liquid carbon quantum dots.

A certain mass of Zn2SnO4 powder was dissolved in 20 ml DI water, and added a certain proportion of carbon quantum dot liquid into the solution. Then the materials were reacted with the help of ultrasonic waves for 30 min, after which the resulting liquid was washed by centrifugation and dried to obtain CQDs@Zn2SnO4 composites.

The sensor consists of a base and a ceramic gas sensor. Fig. 1 shows the environment of testing. The Methanol (99%), Triethylamine (99%), HCOOH (99%), Aniline (99%), HCHO (37%), Benzene (99%) and Toluene (99.7%) gases required in the experiment are all volatile gases. For the test, the volatile saturated gas in the liquid bottle of the corresponding volume of gas was extracted using a syringe and injected into the gas chamber through the injection port. Make sure the gas chamber is closed when injecting. The temperature environment required during the test is provided by a heating wire interspersed inside the tube. Cover the sensor evenly with the material, covering the metal electrode, to ensure maximum uniform distribution to exclude the influence of non-experimental factors.

The materials’ elemental composition and phase structure were investigated by XRD-7000 from Hitachi, the device information are Cu-Kα radiation, the wavelength λ is 1.5406 Å, and the microstructure of the materials was determined by observation using Gemini 500 SEM electron microscope from Zeiss, the chemical valence and binding energy of the elements were determined by XPS, the microstructure of the composites were further investigated by TEM, and the mapping distribution of the elements was examined. The energy bands of the samples can be calculated from the data of UV–Vis and the functional group species of the original materials and composites were confirmed by using FTIR.

The prepared ceramic tube gas sensor is placed into a closed gas chamber, the synthetic air is connected through a pipe, and the triethylamine gas is injected by a syringe to ensure the concentration. S is the response value of the materials to the gas and it is obtained by the following equation.
(1)
S =
R a
R g

Ra and Rg in this equation represent the sensing material's resistance in air and triethylamine environments. The reaction time which is the response/recovery time of the sensor is the time taken to reach 90% of the change in resistance.
The XRD data of the pristine Zn2SnO4 material and the composites are shown in Fig. 2 .

The data show that multiple peaks at 29.1°, 34.5°, 35.8°, 41.9°, 55.1°, and 60° can be observed in the range of 2θ angles from 10 to 60°, and the synthesized material is very similar to the Zn2SnO4 standard card (JCPDS card no. 24–1470) [26], which proves that octahedral anti-spinel Zn2SnO4 was successfully fabricated. It can be seen that the smoothness of the curve is best when the concentration of CQDs is 1 wt%, which indicates that the composites are better crystallized at this concentration. There are no obvious peaks of carbon elements in Fig. 2. The carbon quantum dots will be shown in the TEM data.

The spectrum data of the 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composite is shown in Fig. 3 (a), which proves the presence of elements of Zn, Sn, C, and O in the composite. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the peaks located at 486.18 eV and 494.56 eV prove the Sn element is present in Sn4+. Two peaks located at 1021.22 eV and 1044.21 eV in Fig. 3(c), prove that the element Zn is present in the material with the valence state of Zn2+. Fig. 3(d) shows the O 1s fine spectra of Zn2SnO4 and Fig. 3(e) shows the spectra of 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4. By comparison, it is possible to know that the content of lattice oxygen (OL) decreases, but the oxygen vacancies (OV) and adsorbed oxygen (OC) increase after the composite of CQDs, which is due to the combination of CQDs, the composite's specific surface area increased, and the material has added more adsorption sites. The synthesized material was further characterised by BET. Fig. 3(f) shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of 1 wt% CODs@Zn2SnO4. A clear hysteresis loop can be observed, which represents the typical IV isotherm behaviour. The specific surface area of the synthesized material was calculated to be 9.51 m2/g. This is due to the fact that the synthesized material is an octahedral smooth surface material and does not have holes and gaps, so the specific surface area is not too large.

The equation to calculate the forbidden band of the direct band gap using the data obtained by UV–Vis of Fig. 4 (a), (b) is:
(2)
α h v = A
(
h v −
E g
)
2
The resulting forbidden bandwidths of Zn2SnO4 and 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 are 3.15 eV and 3.09 eV, respectively, show that the material has a narrower forbidden bandwidth due to the introduction of CQDs.

The FTIR curves of the pristine Zn2SnO4 and the best composite are shown in Fig. 4(c). The difference is that the composites have distinct characteristic peaks located at the coordinates 1144.69 and 3384.47, which indicate the vibrations between the –CO bonds and between –OH within the composites, respectively. It shows that the composites have a richer variety and a greater number of functional groups due to the combination of CQDs.

The microstructure and elemental distribution of the material can be obtained from Fig. 5 . Fig. 5(a) shows that Zn2SnO4 exhibits an octahedral morphology. Fig. 5(b)(c)(d) shows the material's microstructure that was observed by TEM. It can be seen that the CQDs with diameters within ∼5 nm are closely attached to the surface of Zn2SnO4 and the lattice distance is ∼0.29 nm, which is similar to the lattice length of carbon quantum dots mentioned in previous studies [27]. Moreover, the carbon quantum dots are round and uniformly distributed, and there is almost no aggregation of quantum dots, which greatly improves the composite's specific surface area. In this work, to more accurately determine the distribution status of the elements, the mapping localization of the elements was performed for the 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composite and obtained Fig. 5(e)–5(j). Fig. 5(j) shows that the content of CQDs is the least on the whole.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the variation of Ra for all materials at different temperatures. The sensitive material was covered onto the sensor using the drop coating method, and the formed films ranged from 30 μm to 100 μm [28,29]. In addition, since the sensor in this study is a surface-controlled sensor based on the surface reaction, the effect on performance brought by the film thickness is acceptable within the above-acceptable film thickness range. At the same temperature, as the concentration of CQDs within the material increases, the contact interface between the two materials becomes larger, and in the interface region, the resistive transfer between the two materials may be hindered and the resistance becomes larger, while the interface defects increase with the increase in concentration, resulting in higher resistance. The best operating temperature is 270 °C and the response to triethylamine (100 ppm) is at 45. Fig. 6(b) shows more details of the resistance of the 1 wt% CQDs@Zn2SnO4 and pure Zn2SnO4 when the TEA into the chamber. After the combination of CQDs with the original material, the response/recovery times are reduced to 18 s and 29 s, respectively, while the response is improved from 8 to about 45. The I–V curves of the best composite are shown in Fig. 6(c). The resistance of CQDs@Zn2SnO4 decreases with increasing operating temperature. The sensor is almost non-conductive at low temperatures. Above 150 °C, the sensor current starts to change significantly.

The fitting curve and trend of the detection response of the 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composite for the concentration gradient of triethylamine are shown in Fig. 7 (a), showing the high linearity of the sensor response performance. Fig. 7(b) shows that the material's detection limit for triethylamine is 1 ppm, and the response value is 1.5. The highest response value can reach about 50 for 100 ppm of triethylamine.

Fig. 8 (a), (b) shows the selectivity of 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composites for different kinds of gases, respectively. Compared to other types of gases (e.g. alcohols, benzene, aldehydes, amines, acids), the sensor response value for triethylamine is more than 10 times higher. In gas selectivity tests, the sensor demonstrated high selectivity for triethylamine. The reason for this can be explained in terms of Lewis acid-base interactions and electrophilicity: firstly, triethylamine can act as a Lewis base because the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom can be donated to Lewis acid sites. In Zn2SnO4 materials, more oxygen vacancies are provided to the material due to the introduction of carbon quantum dots. In the composites, there are a large number of hydroxyl groups (OH-), which also provide a large number of acidic oxygen vacancies on the surface of the composite material. Thus the Lewis acid-base interaction may be the reason for the higher selectivity of the sensor to triethylamine. Secondly, the oxygen atoms on the surface of Zn2SnO4 are electrophilic and the lone pair of electrons of the three ethyl groups and the basic nitrogen in the triethylamine molecule are also electrophilic, thus mutual attraction occurs. This may also be one of the reasons for the high selectivity of the sensor to triethylamine. Due to the introduction of carbon quantum dots, the composites are more selective to triethylamine.

Table 1 lists the sensing performance of different sensor materials for 100 ppm TEA. It can be seen that the response is between 150% and 500% of the other materials in comparison [30–32]. The response/recovery time is also less, up to 1/5 shorter compared to MoS2/ZnO [30], while the material has a lower detection limit. This is due to the high electron transfer efficiency and large surface area of CQDs.

Stability tests and reproducibility tests were performed on 1 wt% CQDs@ Zn2SnO4 composite. Fig. 9 (a) demonstrates the stability of 100 ppm triethylamine over 30 days, with a response dynamically balanced between 45 and 55. It shows that the sensor performance is relatively stable, which is also due to the chemical inertness of CQDs, which are less prone to degradation [33]. The repeatability test of the sensor to 100 ppm triethylamine under the same conditions is shown in Fig. 9(b), with a relatively similar curve, indicating that the sensor has excellent repeatability.

Most of the widely accepted sensing mechanisms of gas sensors are the theory of resistive modulation. When the sensitive material is contacting with air, the oxygen combines with the electrons on the composite's surface to form O−, as shown in Fig. 10 and Eq. (3) [34].
(3)
O 2
(
a d s
)
−
+
e −
→ 2
O
(
a d s
)
−
(
150
°C < T < 400
°C
)

At the end of the reaction, on the material surface, a depletion layer is formed. When triethylamine enters, it first adsorbs on the material surface, forming an adsorbed state, and then reacts with the O− present on the material surface, releasing electrons, as shown in Eq. (4) and (5) [35].
(4)
(
C 2
H 5
)
3
N
(
g a s
)
→
(
C 2
H 5
)
3
N
(
a d s
)
(5)
2
(
C 2
H 5
)
3
N
(
a d s
)
+
(
39 + 2 x
)
O
(
a d s
)
−
→
2 N O
x
+
12 C O
2
+
15 H
2
O +
(
39 + 2 x
)
e
−
At this time, the electrons in the material increase, and the resistance decrease.

In Fig. 11 , under conjugation conditions, electrons inside the composite jump from one state to another state which is from n state to Π state, and Π bonds with stronger electron mobility are formed inside the materials [36,37], reducing the response time of the composite and improving the electrical conductivity [38]. In addition, the bonding between the surface atoms of the carbon quantum dot and the front-line orbitals of the ligand forms a new set of molecular orbitals, thus overcoming the problem of electron or hole traps posed by the quantum dot, reducing the traps and substantially improving the electron transfer efficiency [39,40].

In summary, octahedral binary metal oxide Zn2SnO4 materials was prepared with the hydrothermal synthesis method, then modified with the ∼5 nm CQDs, which were combined to form a trace triethylamine detection sensor with high performance. The work of combining carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with binary metal oxides for the detection of triethylamine is the first application in gas sensing. Carbon quantum dots have the advantages of low-cost and high electron transfer efficiency, composites modified by it have achieved over 600% improvement in response value. This work could provide a feasible approach for high performance quantum dot type gas sensors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62071112 and 62033002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities in China (N2201008 and N2304024), the 111 Project (B16009) and the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (F2020501040).
